title: Genesis 23
subtitle: The Death of Sarah; the Purchase of Machpelah

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — Sarah Dies; Abraham Negotiates for a Burial Place

poi.name: Hebron / Kiriath Arba
poi.text: Hebron (also called Kiriath Arba) is where Sarah dies at 127 years old. Abraham is "a foreigner and stranger" here — he owns no ground in the Promised Land at this point. The death of the covenant matriarch in a land the family doesn't possess dramatises the gap between promise and possession.

notes.note: MacArthur: Sarah died at 127 years old -- the only woman whose age at death is recorded in Scripture. Abraham mourned and wept for her. The covenant man's grief is not suppressed but expressed. The weeping is followed immediately by practical action to honour her burial -- grief and responsibility together.

MacArthur: Abraham bows to the Hittites and requests a burial site 'among you' -- he identifies himself as 'a foreigner and stranger among you' (v.4). Despite the covenant promise of the land, Abraham lived in Canaan as an alien throughout his lifetime (Heb 11:13). His faith was in the promise, not in present possession.

heb.paragraph: Sarah's death (v.2) in Kiriath Arba/Hebron begins the process of covenant land acquisition. The problem is stark: Abraham owns nothing — he is a ger wĕtôšāb ("stranger and sojourner," v.4) in the land God has promised him. The first legal transaction of Genesis will be for a burial ground — a small piece of the promised land, legally purchased at full price, so that covenant family can be buried in it. The burial ground is the down payment on the full land promise.

The elaborate social negotiation of vv.5-16 — the "no, you honour us too much" and counter-offers of Hittite hospitality protocol — reflects the high-context culture of ancient Near Eastern land transactions. The Hittites address Abraham as "a mighty prince (nĕśîʾ ʾĕlōhîm) among us" (v.6) — literally "a prince of God," acknowledging his special status. Abraham must navigate both the social requirement of accepting hospitality and the legal necessity of outright ownership, not a gift that could be reclaimed by any heir.

"Abraham agreed to Ephron's terms and weighed out for him the price he had named" (v.16) — the commercial language of weighing silver is precise legal terminology. The transaction is public (witnessed at the city gate, v.18), at full market value, permanently recorded ("according to the current commercial standard"). Abraham refuses a gift specifically to prevent future legal challenges to ownership. The cave of Machpelah, legally purchased, becomes Israel's first title deed to the promised land — a covenant-in-miniature of the full land promise.

panels.hist: The legal detail of Genesis 23 closely mirrors Hittite and Mesopotamian property transaction documents. The public negotiation at the city gate, the witnesses, the careful recording of the price, and the formula "in the hearing of the sons of Heth" correspond exactly to ANE legal practice for real property transfer. Scholars like M.R. Lehmann have noted that the Hittite legal code (c. 1300 BC) required the seller to pay feudal obligations if he sold only part of his land — which explains why Ephron insists on selling the whole field (v.11), not just the cave. Abraham wisely accepts.
panels.ctx: The death of Sarah is recorded with deliberate economy — one verse (v.2) covering her death, Abraham's grief, and the transition to the business of burial. The chapter's length is not callousness but theological purpose: the real estate transaction is the point. Abraham is purchasing the first permanent title to the promised land — a small cave and a field, but the beginning of possession. He dies owning only this plot, yet died in faith.

cross.note: Jacob later asks to be buried at Machpelah with Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, and Leah. The purchase here secures the only piece of the promised land the patriarchs actually own — and it becomes the family tomb for three generations.

Hebrews cites the patriarchs as strangers who 'were longing for a better country — a heavenly one.' Abraham's need to buy burial land in the very land promised to him underscores the not-yet dimension of faith.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarah's death at age 127 is the only woman's lifespan recorded in Genesis — a signal of her unique standing as the matriarch of the covenant. Sarna: "The negotiation for a burial site reveals Abraham's legal status in Canaan: he is a ger vetoshav — a resident alien, a foreigner with long-term permission to dwell but without land rights. The entire chapter is a legal document demonstrating how Abraham acquired the only piece of Canaan he ever owned outright."

The Hittite elders acknowledge Abraham as 'a mighty prince among us' despite his self-identification as a foreigner and stranger. Sarna notes the tension: Abraham is simultaneously the covenant heir of the land and a sojourner with no legal standing. The purchase of Machpelah is his only legally acquired land in Canaan.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abraham bows to the Hittites and asks for a burial cave. Alter: "The social choreography of the negotiation is meticulously observed: Abraham bows, uses formal address, makes a request. The Hittites respond with generous overstatement ('you are a prince of God among us; bury your dead in the choicest of our tombs'). The exchange follows the ancient Near Eastern conventions of honorific bargaining."

Abraham's specific request — the cave at the edge of Ephron's field — is unusually precise. Alter notes he asks for the minimum necessary: not the whole field but its edge, not a new burial site but the specific cave already there. The precision signals careful planning.

Abraham describes himself as a "foreigner and stranger" in Canaan. Calvin: "Although Abraham had dwelt long in the land, he calls himself a stranger. This teaches us that we also are strangers in this world, and must not fix our minds here, but aspire to the heavenly country." The patriarch's status as alien in the promised land is a theological paradigm for the believer's status in the world.

Calvin: Abraham was an alien yet asked with the confidence of a man with a fixed abode. This confidence was not presumption but faith — he acted as heir of the promise because he was the heir of the promise, even though possession was still future.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on ger vetoshav ("alien and stranger") explains this is a technical legal term for resident foreigner status in the ancient Near East — someone with permission to dwell but without full citizen rights, including land ownership. The note observes that Abraham's request for a burial site is a request for the one form of permanent land acquisition available to a resident alien.

The NET note on ger vetoshav (alien and stranger) explains this technical term describes someone without tribal land rights — no legal standing to own or inherit property. The irony: Abraham is promised the whole land but must formally purchase a small cave because he has no legal land rights as an alien resident.

poi.name: Machpelah / Cave of Machpelah
poi.text: Machpelah is the first piece of the Promised Land Abraham legally owns — purchased at full market price from Ephron the Hittite. The cave becomes the family tomb: Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah are all buried here. The patriarchal claim on Canaan is rooted in this legally acquired burial ground.

notes.note: MacArthur: The 400 shekels of silver at the going merchant rate establishes a legal, documented purchase -- not a gift or a loan. Abraham insists on paying full market value. The first piece of land in the covenant inheritance is acquired through a legally binding commercial transaction that cannot be disputed or reclaimed.

MacArthur: 'The field and the cave in it were deeded to Abraham by the Hittites as a burial site' -- the legal conclusion of the transaction. The cave of Machpelah at Hebron becomes the burial site for Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Sarah, Rebekah, and Leah (49:31; 50:13). The tiny plot of legally-owned land is the first fulfilment of the land promise.

heb.paragraph: The cave (mĕʿārāh) of Machpelah will become the family burial place of the covenant line: Sarah (23:19), Abraham (25:9), Isaac and Rebekah (35:27-29), Leah (49:31), and Jacob (50:13). The cave functions as the family's covenant marker in the land — their bodies are buried there because they hold the promise of resurrection and return to the land. Jacob's insistence from Egypt that he be buried in Machpelah (49:29-32) and Joseph's anticipation of an Exodus (50:24-25) both depend on this theology: the covenant land is where covenant bodies belong.

heb.gloss: property for a burial site / burial holding
heb.paragraph: Abraham's request is for ʾăḥûzat qeber — "a burial holding" (v.4, 9, 20). The term ʾăḥûzāh (holding/property) appears in the land legislation of Leviticus 25 as the permanent family landholding that cannot be permanently alienated. By purchasing burial ground, Abraham is establishing an ʾăḥûzāh — a permanent covenant landholding — in the land of promise before any of the larger promise is fulfilled. The burial ground is the eschatological guarantee: the covenant family will rise and possess this land.

The Hittites of Hebron are listed among the Canaanite peoples whose land Israel will eventually inherit (10:15; 15:20). Their respectful treatment of Abraham — "a mighty prince among us" (v.6) — demonstrates that the covenant patriarch has earned a standing even among the resident peoples of the land. The commercial transaction at the gate of Hebron is witnessed by the very people who will ultimately be displaced by Abraham's descendants — a legal irony embedded in the covenant narrative.

panels.hist: The legal detail of Genesis 23 closely mirrors Hittite and Mesopotamian property transaction documents. The public negotiation at the city gate, the witnesses, the careful recording of the price, and the formula "in the hearing of the sons of Heth" correspond exactly to ANE legal practice for real property transfer. Scholars like M.R. Lehmann have noted that the Hittite legal code (c. 1300 BC) required the seller to pay feudal obligations if he sold only part of his land — which explains why Ephron insists on selling the whole field (v.11), not just the cave. Abraham wisely accepts.
panels.ctx: The death of Sarah is recorded with deliberate economy — one verse (v.2) covering her death, Abraham's grief, and the transition to the business of burial. The chapter's length is not callousness but theological purpose: the real estate transaction is the point. Abraham is purchasing the first permanent title to the promised land — a small cave and a field, but the beginning of possession. He dies owning only this plot, yet died in faith.

cross.note: Stephen references the patriarchal burials in his speech, connecting the land purchases to God's faithfulness. The Machpelah transaction is legal evidence of covenant continuity.
Both Isaac and Ishmael bury Abraham at Machpelah — the brothers reunited at the grave. The purchased cave becomes a site of reconciliation as well as grief.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The formal purchase of Machpelah at Ephron's asking price (400 shekels of silver) is confirmed as a legal transaction "in the hearing of all who had come to the gate" — the legal witnesses. Sarna: "Every element of the transaction follows ancient Near Eastern legal conventions: public negotiation at the city gate, the weighing of silver (not coins — currency did not exist), the formal description of the property, the public confirmation. The chapter is a carefully preserved legal record."

The burial of Sarah at Machpelah "in the land of Canaan" is repeated twice in the closing verses. Sarna: "The emphasis on Canaan is deliberate: this tomb in the promised land is the first secure foothold of the covenant. Abraham has purchased what he was promised. The cave will become the family tomb — Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Leah, and Jacob will all be buried here."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ephron names his price — 400 shekels — with a dismissive flourish: "What is that between me and you? Bury your dead." The social convention is to make the price seem trivial. Alter: "Abraham weighs out the silver immediately, without haggling. The man who argued from 50 to 10 righteous people in chapter 18 does not bargain here. He pays the full price named. The tomb that will hold the mother of the covenant is purchased without negotiation."

The legal precision of the transaction — field, all trees within all boundaries, specified as Abraham's property in the presence of all the Hittites at the gate — is the most elaborate land transaction in Genesis. Alter notes the formality establishes Machpelah as unambiguously Abraham's permanent legal property.

The legal precision of the purchase is a testimony of faith. Calvin: "Abraham did not merely show patience in his affliction but even a heroic magnanimity of spirit. He purchases a sepulchre in the land of promise, as a pledge and earnest that the time would come when his posterity would obtain possession of the whole land." The tomb is the first installment on the promise.

Calvin: Moses is exact in describing the purchase because this cave was a pledge of the whole land. The burial place was the first instalment of the inheritance. Every detail is preserved because the covenant's fulfilment depends on the legal reality of this transaction.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "400 shekels of silver, according to the weight current among the merchants" provides historical context: this is a significant sum. The note observes this is the first precisely documented financial transaction in the Bible and notes the pre-coin method: silver weighed by standard merchant weights. The legal precision reflects an authentic ancient Near Eastern transaction.

The NET note traces the ancient Near Eastern conveyance formula: property described, price stated, witnesses named, transfer formalised. The note compares Jeremiah's purchase of a field during the siege of Jerusalem (Jer 32:9-15) as a parallel act of covenant faith.

ppl.name: Sarah
ppl.text: The only woman in the Bible whose age at death is recorded (127 years). She is also the only woman in the Bible to have a chapter devoted to her burial — a mark of her status as covenant matriarch. Her death occasions the only piece of land Abraham legally owns.

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: Mourns genuinely ("went in to mourn...and to weep"), then rises to handle practical necessity with legal precision. His insistence on paying full price rather than accepting a gift ensures uncontestable title — a model of integrity in commercial dealings.

ppl.name: Ephron the Hittite
ppl.text: His initial generosity ("I give it to you") is standard ANE negotiating courtesy — not a genuine offer. When Abraham persists, Ephron names a high price. The transaction is entirely realistic and documented in accordance with Hittite legal practice.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"I am a foreigner and stranger among you. Sell me some property for a burial site here so I can bury my dead."ESV"I am a sojourner and foreigner among you; give me property among you for a burying place, that I may bury my dead out of my sight."KJV"I am a stranger and a sojourner with you: give me a possession of a buryingplace with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight."

src.title: Hittite Legal Code (c. 1300 BC)
src.quote: The Hittite law code required that when an entire property was sold, all feudal obligations transferred to the buyer. Ephron's insistence on selling the whole field (not just the cave) legally transferred all obligations to Abraham — his phrasing "I give it to you" followed by quoting the full price follows standard Hittite negotiating protocol exactly.
src.note: M.R. Lehmann (1953) argued this match between Genesis 23 and Hittite law is evidence for the antiquity and historical accuracy of the text.

rec.text: The author of Hebrews reads Abraham's status as a "stranger and foreigner" (his own words in Gen 23:4) as a theological statement about the nature of faith: "These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth."

rows.label: The Negotiation: Covenant Faith as Legal Diplomacy
rows.text: Abraham bows to the Hittites and conducts elaborate negotiations for a single cave. The diplomacy is covenant faith enacted: he acts as heir of the land by formally purchasing his first legal foothold in it.

rows.label: Machpelah: Dead Buried in Unpossessed Land
rows.text: Abraham buried Sarah in Machpelah -- the first covenant-family burial in the Promised Land. The dead are laid down in the land they did not possess alive. Faith claims the future by burying its dead in it.

hebtext.note: Sarah's death (v.2) in Kiriath Arba/Hebron begins the process of covenant land acquisition. The problem is stark: Abraham owns nothing — he is a ger wĕtôšāb ("stranger and sojourner," v.4) in the land God has promised him. hebtext.note: Abraham's request is for ʾăḥûzat qeber — "a burial holding" (v.4, 9, 20). The term ʾăḥûzāh (holding/property) appears in the land legislation of Leviticus 25 as the permanent family landholding that cannot be permanently alienated. positions.name: Anachronistic / literary
positions.argument: The Hittite empire was centred in Anatolia; no evidence of Hittites in Hebron in the patriarchal period. "Hittites" (bene-het) may be a later retroactive designation.

positions.name: Local Hittites / Neo-Hittites
positions.argument: The term "Hittite" is used broadly in the OT for Canaanite sub-groups (cf. Uriah the Hittite in Hebron's region). Smaller Hittite-related groups may have been present in Canaan earlier than the main empire. The legal procedures in ch.23 match Hittite land-purchase texts of the 2nd millennium BCE.
